coredata.dc:title: Machine learning approaches to predict adsorption performance of sugarcane derived-carbon dot −based composite in the removal of dyes

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a sustainable and low-cost approach to uniformly blend nanofillers (TiO2) and CDs) into chitosan polymer matrices, aiming to enhance the adsorption capacity and regeneration efficiency of the composite. The effect of degree of deacetylation on the composite was examined using crosslinkers EDA and ECH, achieving a DD of 78.8 % and demonstrating improved chemical and thermal stability compared to chitosan alone. Furthermore, SEM, TEM, and EDX mapping confirmed successful dispersion of nanofillers within the crosslinked chitosan matrix, with excellent adsorption performance towards cationic and anionic dyes (1666.67 mg/g and 1630 mg/g, respectively). The study also presents a novel approach that employs machine learning techniques to accurately predict optimization results. This study combines experiments and AI techniques using Python and machine learning algorithms on a dataset of over 600 experiments, achieving high forecast accuracy with the Random Forest model, evidenced by an R2 value of 1 and the lowest mean squared error for both dyes. The adsorption process followed Freundlich and the pseudo-second-order model. The statistical physical model explained a mixed orientation of anionic dye molecules and a multi-molecular mechanism of cationic dye molecules over nanocomposite, with FTIR results indicating electrostatic attraction, π-π interaction, and hydrogen bonding as key factors in the adsorption process. The composite material used in the study showed a consistent ability to adsorb dyes over 5 regeneration cycles in the column adsorption study, indicating its reusability. The results of this comprehensive investigation contribute to advancing sustainable wastewater treatment through the development of innovative nanocomposites and the use of sophisticated modelling techniques.

head.ce:title: Machine learning approaches to predict adsorption performance of sugarcane derived-carbon dot −based composite in the removal of dyes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a sustainable and low-cost approach to uniformly blend nanofillers (TiO2) and CDs) into chitosan polymer matrices, aiming to enhance the adsorption capacity and regeneration efficiency of the composite. The effect of degree of deacetylation on the composite was examined using crosslinkers EDA and ECH, achieving a DD of 78.8 % and demonstrating improved chemical and thermal stability compared to chitosan alone. Furthermore, SEM, TEM, and EDX mapping confirmed successful dispersion of nanofillers within the crosslinked chitosan matrix, with excellent adsorption performance towards cationic and anionic dyes (1666.67 mg/g and 1630 mg/g, respectively). The study also presents a novel approach that employs machine learning techniques to accurately predict optimization results. This study combines experiments and AI techniques using Python and machine learning algorithms on a dataset of over 600 experiments, achieving high forecast accuracy with the Random Forest model, evidenced by an R2 value of 1 and the lowest mean squared error for both dyes. The adsorption process followed Freundlich and the pseudo-second-order model. The statistical physical model explained a mixed orientation of anionic dye molecules and a multi-molecular mechanism of cationic dye molecules over nanocomposite, with FTIR results indicating electrostatic attraction, π-π interaction, and hydrogen bonding as key factors in the adsorption process. The composite material used in the study showed a consistent ability to adsorb dyes over 5 regeneration cycles in the column adsorption study, indicating its reusability. The results of this comprehensive investigation contribute to advancing sustainable wastewater treatment through the development of innovative nanocomposites and the use of sophisticated modelling techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of wastewater through environmentally friendly techniques that degrade harmful organic compounds is essential. An efficient approach includes the utilization of specific adsorbents, with polymer nanocomposites (PNC) being particularly notable for their ability to harness the advantages of both polymers and nanomaterials. This makes them adaptable and effective tools for addressing wastewater treatment challenges [1–3]. Polymers are advantageous due to their diverse structures, adaptable surface chemistry, and mechanical strength, making them suitable for prolonged use in various environments. Researchers have encountered difficulties in uniformly blending inorganic or organic nanofillers into polymer matrices and in the process of regeneration [4]. Integrating these nanofillers into the polymer can be achieved by creating nanomaterials directly on the surface of the polymer during polymerization or by blending monomers with pre-existing nanomaterials. Chitosan, derived from chitin, is a commonly used biopolymer in treating wastewater. It is produced by removing acetyl groups from chitin and possesses active sites such as amino and hydroxyl groups for adsorbing organic dyes. Its performance in adsorbing anionic dyes is particularly notable due to its propensity to easily pick up protons in water. The effectiveness of chitosan in dye adsorption can be modified by altering its degree of deacetylation (DD)[5]. However, there is still a need for further research to explore the impact of the degree of deacetylation on the performance of chitosan within composite materials. Therefore, this investigation utilizes ethylenediamine (EDA) and epichlorohydrin (ECH) as crosslinkers with chitosan in order to create chitosan with higher DD.

Moreover, titanium oxide (TiO2) was chosen as filler particles due to their high chemical stability, biocompatibility, low cost, and abundant nature [6]. Apart from its ability to perform photocatalysis, TiO2 shows promising application in water treatment because it adds porosity to composite [7,8]. Studies show that incorporating TiO2 into chitosan enhances the composite's mechanical, physicochemical, thermal, and UV protection properties [8,9]. However, an excessive amount of TiO2 in the biopolymer blend may cause the aggregation of inorganic nanoparticles on the surface of the composite, impacting its mechanical properties [10]. A chitosan/TiO2 membrane has been developed that displayed a high adsorption capacity for removing Cu (II) and Pb (II) ions from water [11]. The investigation by Chien et al. [12] confirmed that chitosan/TiO2 composites have adsorption capabilities, although the capacity was not particularly impressive. The incorporation of carbon dots has the potential to enhance the stability, adsorption capacity, and aggregation of TiO2 nanoparticles within a biopolymer matrix. Furthermore, carbon dots are readily derived from carbon-based materials, demonstrating exceptional optical characteristics and flexibility across diverse applications. The π configuration of carbon dots amplifies their capability for pollutant degradation and improved adsorption of organic dyes [13]. Furthermore, the robust chemical bond between dots and TiO2 enhances stability and mitigates aggregation challenges following crosslinking with chitosan.

Modeling and optimization are crucial in environmental processes because they enhance the system's efficiency without significantly increasing costs [14,15]. The process of adsorption is intricate, involving complex interactions between variables and non-linear behavior of related processes. Hence, identifying the most effective experimental conditions is crucial for achieving optimal efficiency. While classical methods are comprehensive, they can be time-consuming and may only account for certain parametric effects or combined physicochemical interactions [16]. To overcome these challenges, researchers have explored artificial intelligence techniques to understand the nonlinear patterns in pollutant removal through various treatment methods [16]. Various algorithms have been employed in constructing empirical models for adsorption, including Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), the Adaptive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision trees (DTs), random forests (RF), gradient boosting decision trees (GBDTs), and extreme gradient boosting (XGB) [17]. Recent advancements in tree-based models have gained popularity and are increasingly being utilized in scientific research. These models offer the advantage of handling fewer parameters, tolerating errors, and managing diverse variable characteristics [18]. In this investigation, experimental data on dye adsorption using the synthesized composite were used to train models for predicting the composite's adsorption performance. Various factors such as stirring speed, temperature, dosage, pH level, initial dye concentration, and contact time were considered as inputs for model training. The primary goal of this study is to develop algorithms that can evaluate the adsorption capacity of composites utilizing different functionalization methods while also examining the impact of each parameter on dye uptake.

The present study aims to fabricate functionalized chitosan through the impregnation of TiO2 nanofiller and Carbon Dots (CDs). Additionally, the synthesis of CDs from sugarcane bagasse using solvothermal treatment has been disclosed. The study also aimed to investigate the efficacy of the adsorption process and its mechanism through equilibrium, kinetic, and thermodynamic analyses. Moreover, it sought to compare machine-learning models for simulating adsorption.

Chitosan was purchased from Otto Chemie Pvt Ltd. Titanium oxide nanoparticles (≥99 %, TiO2) were purchased from Merck, India. EDA (99 %) and ECH (99 %) were purchased from Fisher scientific, India. Brilliant green BG (∼90 %, C27H34N2O4S), and methyl red MR (≥85 %, C15H15N3O2) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (97 %, NaOH), hydrochloric acid, (35–37 %, HCl), and potassium nitrate (KNO3) were purchased from Merck, India. Ethanol (>99 %, C2H5OH) was purchased from Fine chemicals. Dilution of the dye solution and washing of the adsorbent were performed using double-distilled water (DDW).

Sugarcane bagasse was dehydrated in an oven overnight, then finely ground to produce CD. The resulting particles were combined with a 75:25 v/v ratio of DDW:C2H5OH and placed in a heated autoclave reactor at 200 ◦C for 5 h. Next, the dark brown solution goes through centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min and filtration using a 0.45 µm filter after cooling from the autoclave.

To synthesize TiO2-CD, the composite was obtained by mixing together 120 mL DDW and 35 mL C2H5OH with 3 g TiO2 and varying amounts of CD (ranging from to 2–10 mL). This mixture was stirred for 4 h before being transferred into an autoclave reactor heated at 140 ◦C for 4 h. After centrifuging it at 7000 rpm for 10 min, washing thoroughly with DDW, and drying out in a vacuum at 60 °C for 6 h.

Chitosan hydrolysis was carried out to determine the DD. To do this, 3 g of chitosan was dispersed in a solution containing 2 % v/v acetic acid and stirred until the particles were completely dissolved. A 0.5 M NaOH solution (150 mL) was then added drop by drop to the mixture for periods of 12 h and 24 h. The resulting products were named as ChA and ChB, washed with DDW to eliminate excess NaOH, and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h.

To prepare an EDA-modified chitosan composite, 3 g TiO2-CD was mixed with 3 g CH and 2 % v/v acetic acid and stirred until particles were dissolved completely. Then 0.5 M of 150 mL of NaOH solution was added dropwise and stirred for 1 h. Excess NaOH was washed with distilled water during the filtration process. The CH obtained in this step was dispersed in 300 mL of DDW: C2H5OH (1:1), and then 6 mL of ECH is added. ECH was used as a crosslinker for chitosan to maintain its stability and strength. The solution was stirred for 16 h, and solid phases were separated by centrifugation at 7000 rpm, washed with DDW, and dried in oven 60 °C for 6 h. This product was labelled ECH-CH-TiO2-CD. The obtained product was added in 300 mL of DDW: C2H5OH (1:1), followed by a mixing of 5 mL EDA stirred for 16 h. The obtained product was separated by centrifugation at 7000 rpm, washed with DDW, and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. This product was named as EDA-CH-TiO2-CD. The schematic representation of the synthesis of the chitosan composite is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch adsorption experiments and analysis of variables
ce:section.ce:para:
A stock solution of MR and BG dyes at a concentration of 1000 mg/L was initially prepared and subsequently diluted to a range of 10–1000 mg/L. The calibration curve was generated using the absorbance values obtained from standard solutions, which were measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer set to the maximum wavelength of 545 nm for MR dye and 624 nm for BG dye. Batch mode adsorption studies of the dyes were conducted, with parameters being systematically varied. These parameters are the effect of amount of adsorbent utilized (0.02–0.05 g), pH of dye solution (2–10), temperature of dye solution (30–60 °C), initial dye concentrations (50–1000 mg/L), stirring speed of dye solution (60, 80, and 100 rpm), and contact time (5–60 min and 10–240 min). The adsorption capacity and adsorption efficiency were evaluated using Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively.

where, Ci (mg/L) and Cf (mg/L) are initial and final concentration of dye, V is the volume of dye used (l), and M is the weight of adsorbent (g).

The point of zero charge (pHpzc) of the composite was determined by the solid addition method with a 0.01 M KNO3 solution. Initially, 50 mL of KNO3 solution was placed into ten 100 mL Erlenmeyer flasks. Then, 0.1 g of the PNC was added to each flask. The adjustment of pH was carried out using 0.01 M HCl and NaOH. The flasks were covered and stirred intermittently for 24 h. The final pH values in each flask were recorded. By calculating the difference (ΔpH) between the initial and final pH values for each flask and plotting the initial pH against ΔpH, the pHpzc was determined as the intersection point of the curve with the x-axis.

Continuous adsorption studies were conducted using a borosilicate glass column with a diameter of 10 mm and a length of 150 mm, operating in a downflow mode. The column was loaded with a predetermined amount of the adsorbent, and a synthetic dye solution has flowed through it using a peristaltic pump (Ravel Hiteks, Pvt. Ltd; Model: RH-P100VS-100) at a flow rate ranging from 0.2 to 0.5 mL/min. The effluent dye solution was collected at regular intervals, and its concentration was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Each trial was performed twice, and the outcomes were presented as the mean with their corresponding standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumentation used for characterization of composite
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of composites were analysed for pre and post adsorption by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) using PerkinElmer Spectrometer version 10.4. The crystallinity and amorphous nature of the composite were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Rigaku TTRAX III. The textural and morphological properties of the composite, along with elemental distribution analysis, were examined using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) with the utilization of JEOL-JEM 2100 and JEOL-JSM 6510 instruments. Elemental analysis and oxidation state of the composite were determined by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) using PHI 5000 VersaProbe III. The surface area of the composite was calculated using N2 adsorption–desorption method known as Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (Autosorb I) with a degassing temperature of 100 ℃ for 3 h, and pore size distribution was determined on the basis of Barrett, Joyner, and Halenda (BJH) and density functional theory (DST) method. Thermal analysis of the composite was conducted by Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in a nitrogen atmosphere using a thermogravimetric analyzer (Perkinelmer pyris) at a temperature rate of 20℃/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model selection for adsorption studies of dyes on composite
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, seven types of models were used like Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest (RF), support vector machine (SVM), gradient boosting (GB), Decision trees, extra trees regressor, and extreme gradient boosting (XGB) respectively.

ANNs are a powerful tool for addressing nonlinear relationships in supervised learning [19]. They function similarly to the human brain, learning from data and establishing connections between input and output variables. These connections are built using neurons and weights, and each neuron is linked to the adjacent layer, forming complex relationships. Each neuron within the network receives signals from the input layer, consisting of 'm' neurons. These input signals are multiplied by corresponding weights and then directed to the summing junction within each neuron. Every neuron plays a crucial role in handling signals received through connections by employing a nonlinear activation function to process these signals [20]. A bias function is incorporated into the weighted inputs, resulting in a net input that is passed through a transfer function to generate the output neuron's response. In this study, five different input variables are used to determine the concentration of dyes in the effluent (Cf), the adsorption capacity of composite (mg/g), and the adsorption efficiency of dye (%). The input variables are the amount of adsorbent utilized, pH of dye solution, temperature of dye solution, initial dye concentrations, and contact time. The network topology is determined by exploring factors such as activation function, number of neurons in the hidden layer, and number of hidden layers. Several commonly employed ANN architectures are available, each tailored to specific objectives and structures, including the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), and more. In this study, MLP was used to model composite adsorption performance by predicting dyes' adsorption capacity, final dye concentration, and adsorption efficiency. The Limited-memory Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (L-BFGS) and Adam solver were used to train the data. This solver belonged to the quasi-Newton family of optimization methods [21]. The modelling of the adsorption process was executed in Python 3.6.8, and the Scikit-Learn programming library, version 0.21.3, was employed using the MLPRegressor function. Different activation functions such as the identity, hyperbolic tangent (Tanh), logistic (sigmoid), and rectified linear function (ReLu) functions were tested for better accuracy of the model.

RF is a type of ensemble machine learning algorithm designed to enhance predictive capabilities and reduce model variance. It achieves this by combining multiple decision trees. This is accomplished by generating a set of 'n' decision trees using resampling with replacement (bootstrapping) and then aggregating the results from each tree by calculating their averages [22]. Additionally, the RF algorithm employs random selection of a subset of variables in each tree's development process. This randomization reduces correlations between the variables, subsequently lowering the generalization error [17]. In this model, number of decision trees (n) varied between 100–200, and a maximum number of features or variables in each tree varied between 4–6. The input variables were same as used for ANN modelling. Python 3.6.8 and Scikit-Learn programming library was employed with using RandomForestRegressor function. Similar to ANN model, RF model accuracy also quantified by the MSE and R2.

The Extra Tree Regressor extends traditional decision trees by introducing additional randomness during node splitting, making it suitable for complex datasets and resistant to overfitting [17]. The hyperparameter tuning, optimizing critical factors were conducted such as the number of trees in the ensemble, maximum tree depth, and minimum samples required for node splitting. Implementation were carried out using Python 3.6.8 and the Scikit-Learn programming library. This ensemble method, combined with thorough hyperparameter adjustments, ensures robust predictive performance for our research.

The GB model operates by sequentially training a series of decision trees, with each tree focusing on correcting the errors made by the previous ones, thereby improving predictive accuracy [23]. During the methodology, hyperparameter tuning was conducted to optimize essential settings such as the learning rate, maximum tree depth, and the number of boosting stages. The entire implementation was performed using Python 3.6.8 and the Scikit-Learn programming library.

XGB can be used for both regression and classification prediction tasks. It offers distinct advantages compared to traditional gradient boosting models in several aspects. Firstly, it leverages multithreading techniques for constructing tree structures, resulting in faster model training. Secondly, it has the capability to work with incomplete data directly within the dataset, reducing the time required for data preprocessing [24]. These features make XGBoost a more efficient and faster option when compared to conventional gradient boosting models. Implementation was carried out by XGBregressor from xgboost library using Python 3.6.8.

Decision trees also can be used for both regression and classification prediction tasks. The data is maintained in the root node, which is divided into subset forming multiple internal nodes and terminal nodes also known as leaf nodes. At each node in the decision tree, one or more classes are severed from other classes [25]. In decision tree, processing occurs by moving down of trees from root node to the leaf nodes, this approach is known as “top-down approach”. The splitting of data was carried out on the basis of important aspects and organized into tree like structure. Each node represents the decision on the basis of particular set of input parameters.

SVM uses linear algorithms which creates a hyperplane in such a way that it maximizes the marginal distance to get generalized data. Hyperplane is the plane that divide the data and marginal distance is the distance between two marginals passing through closest point of data from hyperplane [26]. The nearest or closest points from hyperplane is known as support vectors. SVM uses a technique known as SVMkernel, which converts low dimension into high dimensions to classify the data using hyperplane. The kernel functions used in SVM model find an efficient way to separate nonlinear data. Various kernel choices are there such as linear, polynomial, spline radial basis function (RBF), and multilayer perceptron. The regression version of SVM is known as support vector regression (SVR) [17]. In this study, SVR polynomial and SVR RBF were used to predict the adsorption performance of composite. SVM-RBF has the ability to handle non-linearly separable data through the use of the radial basis function kernel, which maps the data into a higher-dimensional space.

The performance of all the models was determined by root mean square error (RMSE), mean square error, average relative error (ARE) and R2. These are defined as follows by Eqs. (3)–(6) [20].

Fig. 2 (a) shows the FTIR spectrum of sugarcane bagasse-based CD (named SGCD) and TiO2-CD composite. The IR peaks of SGCDs at 3440 cm−1 correspond to hydroxyl and amine groups (–OH/NH). Further, a sharp peak at 2980 cm−1 shows the stretching of a methyl group (–CH). The characteristics peak at 1640 cm−1, 1510 cm−1, and 1401 cm−1 represents the stretching of −C = N, −C = O, and COO– groups of SGCD, respectively. The remaining peak at 1013 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O/S = O. Some literature has indicated similar outcomes [27]. The IR spectrum of a combination of TiO2 with SGCD shows a reduction in peaks of –OH/NH, –CH, and −C = N. Also, the broad peak at 603 cm−1 shows the characteristics peaks of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C vibrations [13] indicating the successful incorporation of TiO2 into the sugarcane bagasse-based CD composite.

Fig. 2 (b) displays the results of the FTIR analysis of the hydrolyzed chitosan composite and the modified chitosan composite with ECH and EDA. The stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH groups cause the large peaks at 3440 and 3268 cm−1 present in all chitosan composites. Hydrolyzed samples of chitosans named ChA-TiO2-CD show peaks at 2920, 1646, 1558, 1410, 1312, 1157, and 693 cm−1, which are associated with stretching of –CH, vibration of C = O groups in acetylated unit (C = O-NHR), bending vibration of –NH groups, vibration of –OH, –CH groups, asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C groups and characteristics peak of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C vibrations. Additional peaks at 1077 and 1033 cm−1 are due to C-O group stretching vibrations. Each of these peaks is observable in all chitosan composites. However, in the case of hydrolyzed chitosan, known as ChB-TiO2-CD, the intensity of the peaks diminishes, possibly due to the crystalline sensitivity of the chitosan band [28]. It suggests that the degree of crystallinity has been affected after the hydrolysis of chitosan. The DD are determined from FTIR analysis by taking the absorption band ratio at 1312 and 1410 cm−1. The DD is calculated using the Eq. (7) and Eq. (8) [28]
(7)
DA % =
A 1312
/
A 1410
(8)
DD % = 1 - D A %
where DA% is the degree of acetylation. This
A 1312
/
A 1410
ratio has been used for DA% calculation because it is sensitive to the chemical composition of chitosan. The results of different chitosan DD are shown in Table 1 . The results show that the highest value of DD has been obtained for EDA-CH-TiO2-CD as 78.8 %. The DD, which varies from 0 (chitin) to 100 (fully deacetylated chitin), measures the molar fraction of glucosamine monomeric units in chitosan [29]. Chitosan with higher DD exhibits better adsorptive properties due to the availability of higher –NH2 groups. The molecular weight of chitosan is another element that affects the adsorption effectiveness. Low (40–190 kDa), medium (190–310 kDa), and high-molecular-weight (310–375 kDa) forms of commercial chitosan are available. One thing that happens is that when the monomer unit grows, so do the functional groups. However, the hydrophilic characteristic varies according to molecular weight [30]. Therefore, the molecular weight of the synthesized chitosan composite is determined from its relative viscosity. At room temperature, a capillary viscometer has been used to evaluate the relative viscosity of polymers. The definition of intrinsic viscosity is [η] =

. DD is degree of acetylation. The calculated intrinsic viscosity and molecular weight of chitosans are given in Table 1. The results show a lower molecular weight of 47.68 kDa for EDA-CH-TiO2-CD. Therefore, more amino groups in EDA-CH-TiO2-CD can serve as an active site for the adsorption of contaminants from wastewater. DD % of ECH-CH-TiO2-CD and EDA-CH-TiO2-CD are almost similar; however, the molecular weights of both composites have more significant differences. The lower molecular weight of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD generally results in lower crystallinity of materials, suggesting that composites have higher porosity and higher surface area [32].

The XRD pattern of all TiO2 samples with CDs reveals distinct anatase phase peaks (JCPDS file No. 78–2486), displaying their corresponding (hkl) planes as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Nanoparticle peaks are observed at 25.2, 37.9, 40.08, 53.8, 55, and 62.7° with miller indices of (101), (004), (200), (105), and (211). Similar results have been reported in the literature [33]. No peaks are identified with other TiO2 crystal phases, such as rutile, brookite, or other materials. However, observable peaks corresponding to CDs with miller indices (002) indicate the incorporation of dots onto TiO2 nanoparticles. Furthermore, XRD patterns of different concentrations of dots with TiO2 show little difference in the peaks of dots. CDs with significantly less amount (2 mL) show negligible peaks; however, a higher concentration of dots (8 mL and 10 mL) resulted in the addition of several peaks, which might be due to a change in the structure of the TiO2 composite. The XRD pattern of TiO2 particles with 4 mL dots shows the crystalline nature of the commercial anatase form of TiO2. Similar patterns have been observed in chitosan composites (Fig. 3 (b)), with little difference in the crystal structure of the TiO2 based chitosan composite, showing that the composite synthesis did not damage the distinctive structure of TiO2. Further, very sharp peaks clearly show less/no impurities in the composites. The estimated crystallite size of TiO2 nanoparticles and chitosan composite is 60.24 nm and 149.3 nm.

Fig. 7 (a) displays the survey XPS of spectra of TiO2-CD and chitosan composites. The spectra of TiO2-CD indicate four distinct peaks at energies of 285, 398, and 530 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Ti2p, respectively. The C1s spectra split into three peaks at 284.84, 286, and 288 eV, corresponding to C = C, C-N, and C-OH groups due to the availability of SGCD (Fig. 7 (b)). The N1s spectra indicate the successful impregnation of SGCD with TiO2 nanoparticles. It exhibits split peaks, with one at 399 eV corresponding to C-N and another at 400 eV attributed to N–H (Fig. 7(c)). The nitrogen content in SGCD measures at 3 %. The O1s spectra show binding energy at 529, 530, and 531 eV attributed to the Ti-O network, –OH and C = O groups (Fig. 7(d)). The spectra of Ti2p splits into two peaks corresponding to Ti2p 3/2 and Ti2p 1/2 at 458 eV and 468 eV demonstrating the presence of the metal in the composite in the Ti (IV) state (Fig. 7(e)). The XPS spectra of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD also show four distinct peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Ti2p. The nitrogen content in the composite increases to 7.6 % due to the modification of the composite by ethylenediamine. It shows peaks of C-N-C and –NH groups at 398.8 eV and 400 eV (Fig. 7 (g)). Further, C1s spectra split into three different peaks corresponding to C–C, C-N, and O-C-O at 284.4, 285.7, and 287.5 eV (Fig. 7 (f)). The presence of C-N and O-C-O shows successful impregnation of TiO2-CD over the chitosan composite. The O1s spectra show peaks of the group –OH and C = O peaks at 529.27 and 531.23 eV binding energies, respectively (Fig. 7 (h)).

TGA analysis (Fig. 8 ) assesses the thermal stability of pure chitosan and chitosan modified with ECH and EDA. The TGA curve of pure chitosan and its composite exhibits a slight weight loss below 100 °C, primarily attributed to the evaporation of water molecules. Pure chitosan showed weight loss that began at about 100 °C and increased between 278 and 375 °C. The chitosan breaks down when the temperature reaches 600 °C. Depolymerization of chitosan chains, pyranose ring breakdown via dehydration and deamination, and ring-opening reaction are the causes of this behavior [34]. Chitosan composites demonstrate comparable observations; however, the weight loss is less pronounced than pure chitosan. For the EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite, three steps can be observed (30–100, 100–278, 278–375 °C), where the first phase is due to loss of water, the second phase can be due to the availability of Ti-O network in polymer, the third step is expected to degradation of the polymer chain. Thus, it can be seen that the EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite is more thermally stable than pure chitosan.

BET analysis measures the surface area and pore size of the EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite. The composite has micro and mesopores, according to the adsorption–desorption isotherm and the type-IV isotherm, which leads to capillary condensation in the mesopores at the relative pressure range of 0 to 1. In this case, the desorption path differs from the adsorption path, resulting in multi-molecular layer adsorption and making a hysteresis loop, as shown in Fig S1 (a). This composite exhibits H2-type hysteresis in which the desorption curve has a more significant gradient and a fast-declining inflection point with varied pore size, and the pore diameter distribution is relatively large [35]. The H2-type hysteresis is present in porous adsorbents with an ambiguous distribution of pore sizes and shapes. These findings allow for the formation of disorganized and potentially obstructed holes in the materials produced, as evidenced by the percolation phenomenon [36]. The BET surface area of the composite is 91.85 m2/g. BJH and DFT obtained the pore size distribution plots of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD. The NLDFT approach shows a total pore volume of 0.156 cc/g and a pore size of 14.44 Å (1.444 nm), as shown in Fig S1 (b). Moreover, the BJH pore size distribution shows that the total volume is 0.232 cc/g, and the pore size is 10.097 Å (1.097 nm).

To demonstrate the functions of SGCDs, TiO2-CD, EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite, their UV–vis absorption spectra were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), SGCD, TiO2-CD and EDA-CH-TiO2-CD, respectively. The intensity of absorption decreases with increasing wavelength for all samples. low absorption is obtained in the UV region for pure SGCD because of the addition of TiO2 may lead to surface modifications of the SGCD and there could be energy transfer processes between the SGCD and TiO2. This may result in changes in the absorption and emission characteristics of the composite material.

Fig. 9 (b) illustrates that the most intense emission peak was found at 475 nm with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. A fluorescence study was conducted to explore the optical properties of SGCD at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 9(c)). The results indicate a rise in fluorescence emission intensity as the excitation wavelength increases from 330 to 390 nm, highlighting the strong dependence of SGCD fluorescence on the excitation wavelength. The maximum emission peak is approximately 480 nm, observed at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Fig. 9(d) presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of SGCD, TiO2-CD, and EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite excited at 390 nm. The higher PL intensity indicates a greater recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers. Comparison among all samples, PL spectra reveals that TiO2-CD and EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composites exhibit significant reduction in PL intensity compared to SGCD, suggesting that CD loading can effectively impede photogenerated e− and h+ recombination. Among them, EDA-CH-TiO2-CD composite displays the lowest PL intensity signifying minimum recombination rate for photogenerated e− and h+.

Fig. 10 illustrates the effect of several factors, such as ionic strength, pH, illumination duration, and preservation time, on the PL intensity of SGCDs. Since the chemical stability and photostability of the CDs are crucial for their applications, the effect of different concentrations of NaCl on fluorescence intensity of the SGCDs is explored for the assessment of the ionic strength tolerance of the fluorescent probe. Fig. 10 (a) shows that there is basically no change in PL intensity as the concentration of NaCl increases up to 1 M, indicating that CDs have strong salt resistance. The impact of pH on the PL intensity of CDs is then examined as well. When pH ranges from 1 to 6, the normalized PL intensity of CDs is mostly constant; however, when pH rises from 8 to 12, there is notable drop instability maximum stability shown at pH 7. Fig. 10 (b) presents the results, which suggest that SGCDs may be employed at pH levels within the normal physiological range. Moreover, to further check the photostability and PL intensity of SGCDs after continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp for a different time were investigated (Fig. 10 (c)). Additionally, after 60 days of storage at room temperature and typical light conditions, the PL intensity of SGCDs remains above 90 %, confirming the extraordinary photostability of SGCDs (Fig. 10 (d)). These findings demonstrate that the produced have strong fluorescent stability and can be used for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pearson correlation method of the adsorption of MR and BG dye on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, six different input variables are used for the adsorption of cationic and anionic dyes on composite which include dosage of composite, stirring speed, pH of dye solution, temperature, initial dye concentrations and contact time. The Pearson correlation of MR dye shows that adsorption capacity of composite is positively correlated with stirring speed, pH and temperature of dye solution, and negatively correlated with contact time, adsorbent dosage and initial dye concentration (Fig. 11 (a)). Whereas adsorption capacity of composite for BG dye was found to be positively correlated with stirring speed, time, pH, and initial dye concentration; negatively correlated with temperature, and adsorbent dosage (Fig. 11(b)). The results of Pearson correlation agree with the batch experiments of adsorption. Batch experiments results showed that the maximum adsorption of anionic dye MR has been observed at pH 6 (Fig S2 (a)), representing electrostatic attraction between EDA-CH-TiO2-CD and dye molecules because pHpzc of the composite is 5. The quantity of protonated amine groups reduces with rising pH, which reduces the dye's electrostatic interaction. At pH levels over 7, chitosan chains may acquire a complete negative charge, which further reduces their capacity to attach to anionic sorbates. The competition of OH– ions at pH 8–10 also restricts MR sorption on chitosan sorbents. However, the CH-based composite's ability to adsorb positive ions and cationic contaminants is constraining due to electrostatic repulsion or rivalry with H+ for adsorption sites [37]. However, in this study, the effect of pH on the removal of cationic dye remained the same for all pH values, as shown in Fig S2(b). Even though the pH was initially adjusted appropriately, following the adsorption process, all pH readings showed neural pH. The measurement demonstrated that even at varying pHs, all produced comparable results. It is because, throughout the adsorption procedure, the composite surface stabilized the BG solution into a normal pH. If such is the case, using a composite made of chitosan for actual wastewater effluent would neutralize the discharge effluent (where the pH changes), making it beneficial to know that the synthetic wastewater is neither acidic nor alkaline in condition. Another factor for BG dye adsorption on chitosan nanocomposites may be increased surface area and greater porosity due to increased deacetylation and reduced molecular weight.

At ambient temperature, the single system comprising MR solution with concentrations ranging from 50 to 850 mg/L and BG solution with concentrations ranging from 50 to 700 mg/L has been utilized to investigate the impact of adsorbent dosage on adsorption. The ability of dyes to bind to surfaces reduces as the EDA-CH-TiO2-CD dose increases, as shown in Fig S2 (c,d). The primary reason for the decrease in the adsorption sites in an adsorbent is mainly due to the superposition of the available adsorption sites and the accumulation of adsorbent particles. As mentioned above, this occurrence is likely an initial rationale for the result [38]. The decrease in the composite's adsorption capacity is because of the fewer active sites accessible on the composite compared to the number of dye molecules present.

The adsorption tests at various temperatures have been conducted to analyse the temperature's influence on the process. As the temperature increases from room temperature (30 °C) to 60 °C, the adsorption capacity for both MR and BG dyes decreases across all dye concentrations (Fig S2 (e,f)). It shows that the adsorption process is exothermic. One possible explanation for this is that with increasing temperature, the physical bonds between the dye molecules and the active sites of the adsorbent weaken. Moreover, as the temperature rises, the solubility of the dye in the solvent increases, resulting in stronger interaction forces between the dye and the solvent compared to the forces between the dye molecules and the adsorbent. Consequently, the adsorption of dyes on the composite decreases, displaying an exothermic behaviour.

Further, the effect of contact time and initial dye concentrations shows that adsorption capabilities of MR and BG by EDA-CH-TiO2-CD grew rapidly in the first 10 min, after that became slow over the next 30 min, and eventually reached equilibrium in the last 60 min. Initially, when the dye molecules come into contact with the adsorbent surface, there is rapid dye adsorption due to the availability of vacant adsorption sites. As the contact time increases, the adsorption rate gradually decreases because the number of available sites decreases, and the remaining sites may be more difficult to access and finally reach equilibrium. Furthermore, after a certain contact time, the adsorbent may become saturated, meaning that dye molecules occupy all available adsorption sites on the surface. At this point, further increases in contact time may not lead to significant additional adsorption. It can be seen from the photograph of MR and BG dye (Fig S2 (g) and (h)) before and after adsorption that 50–250 mg/L of MR dye and 50–100 mg/L of BG dye adsorbs completely. However, when the dye concentration rises, more active sites will be swiftly filled by dye molecules until saturation occurs, reducing the composite adsorption capacity. As per the literature, there have been instances where dye removal initially rises and then decreases. According to [39], the initial rise in adsorption occurs by a greater driving force from the water–solid phase, and the following decrease in adsorption due to a greater ratio of dye molecules to active adsorption sites on the adsorbent in the solution. The composite's ability to adsorb to MR and BG enhances as the starting concentration increases. The adsorption efficiency of composite with time at different initial dye concentrations is given in supplementary information (Fig S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of ML algorithms for adsorption of MR and BG dyes on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, machine learning techniques were employed to simulate the adsorption of MR and BG dyes on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD. A dataset comprising 200 experiments (conducted under various conditions in triplicate, totaling 600 experiments) was utilized to construct models for forecasting the final concentration, adsorption capacity (AC, mg/g), and adsorption efficiency (AE, %) using ML algorithms. Subsequently, all the models underwent comparison − a conventional practice in machine learning modeling of the adsorption process. Table 2 shows the best topology obtained for all ML models in predicting the adsorption performance of the composite. Among the four tested activation functions of the ANN model, the ReLu function describes the adsorption more suitably using 6 input and 3 output variables. The predicted and experimental values of all 3 outputs, as shown in Fig S3, are in good agreement with each other.

Similarly, 22 hidden neurons show a good accuracy of the model. RF has two main hyperparameters: the number of trees (ntree) and the maximum number of features (m). Table S1 presents the tuning parameters' range R2, MSE, and RMSE results. The optimal configuration for the RF model for MR dye adsorption is observed when using ntree = 200, m = 6, and no additional options in the forest. In this optimal model, the MSE and RMSE values are 1.722 × 10-24 and 1.3122 × 10-12, respectively, while the R2 values are 1. The predicted and experimental values of all 3 outputs, as shown in Fig S4, are in good agreement with each other. The optimal value of the RF model for BG dye adsorption was also similar to MSE and RMSE values of 1.2600 × 10-14 and 1.1224 × 10-7, respectively, while the R2 values are 1. The optimal hyperparameters obtained for the Extratree regressor model for MR dye are max_depth = 20, max_features = sqrt, min_samples_leaf = 1, min_samples_split = 2, n_estimators = 100 and shows R2, MSE and RMSE values are 0.998, 77.92 and 8.827. However, for BG dye adsorption, the optimal hyperparameters max_depth = 20, max_features = sqrt, min_samples_leaf = 2, min_samples_split = 2, n_estimators = 100 and shows R2, MSE and RMSE values are 0.991, 11.99 and 3.462. As shown in Fig S5, the predicted values of 3 outputs are satisfactory with target values; however, the model shows lower accuracy than the ANN and RF model for both dyes. Decision trees also perform similarly to the extra tree regressor model and deliver lower accuracy than the ANN and RF models. It has been observed that MSE reduces as the hyperparameter (n_estimators) of the GB model increases (as shown in Fig S7). However, the R2 value remains the same, i.e., 1.00. The GB model shows better accuracy than the ANN model. The RF model still offers a higher R2 value and lower MSE than GB. As per the literature, overfitting is one of the challenges if the number of trees increases too much in the GB model; however, in the RF model, too many trees won't cause overfitting. The best hyperparameters for the XGBoost ML model for MR and BG dyes are subsample = 0.9, n_estimators = 200, min_child_weight = 3, max_depth = 7, learning_rate = 0.1, colsample_bytree = 0.8. The MSE and R2 values of the XGBoost model are given in Table 2 and show higher accuracy than the ANN model. The scatter plot of the SVM model for observed and predicted output variables is shown in Fig S10 and Fig S11. The R2 value of SVM with 'rbf' and 'poly' kernel is between 0.7–0.4, almost 30–40 % away from experimental values. The accuracy of the SVM model is the lowest among all ML models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance of the applied models
ce:section.ce:para: Fig S12 illustrates that the violin plots for both MR and BG dyes exhibit symmetry. The adsorption capacity distribution predicted by SVM models has a narrower range than other ML models, with the median of these values being lower in comparison to the other violin plots. Furthermore, it is evident from Fig S11 that SVM models depict a higher concentration of adsorption capacity values approaching zero compared to other ML models. Additionally, the output value distributions provided by RF, extratree, decision trees, GB, XGB, and ANN models are quite similar. The maximum width of violin plots produced by these models is relatively the same at an adsorption capacity of 500 mg/g, whereas the maximum ones obtained near 2000 mg/g, respectively. Similarly, the adsorption efficiency and final concentration distribution for MR and BG dyes are identical for all the models except SVM. Since the shape of most of the violin plots of ML models is similar, a comparison of these models can be performed based on MSE values reported in Table 2. Thus, the RF model shows better accuracy and lower MSE values, outperforming the other models.

The experimental data of MR and BG adsorption by composite was analysed using nonlinear regression with the pseudo-1st-order, pseudo-2nd-order, and intra-particle diffusion models to study the adsorption kinetics. The estimated kinetic constants can be found in Table S3 and Table S4. The results of the trials showed that the pseudo-2nd −order model correlated better with the experimental data because the R2 value of the pseudo-2nd −order model is > 0.99. Moreover, a strong agreement is observed between the calculated data (Qe,cal) and the experimental data (Qe,exp). However, chemical adsorption is assumed to be the rate-limiting step when the pseudo-2nd-order kinetic model is defined. According to the aforementioned findings, the chemisorption process involves a chemical reaction between the dye molecules and the composite. In chemisorption, the adsorbed species form chemical bonds with the surface atoms or molecules of the adsorbent. In contrast to physical adsorption (physisorption), where the adsorbate is retained onto the surface by weaker intermolecular forces such as van der Waals interactions, chemisorption bond creation leads to a stronger connection, which typically involves the sharing, transfer, or exchange of electrons between the adsorbate and adsorbent, leading to the formation of new chemical species.

Moreover, the IPD model also shows higher regression coefficients for all concentrations of dyes. Fig S14 (a) shows the graphs of IPD for the adsorption of MR and BG on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD at different initial dye concentrations, with corresponding constants listed in Table S3 and Table S4. Each plot displays nearly linear portions with positive Y intercepts. If the graph of IPD diffusion (qt vs. t0.5) passes through the origin, it indicates stable IPD, making step (II) the sole rate-limiting step [40]. None of the lines cross the origin, as seen in Fig S14, however, multi-linearity (two linear portions, step I and step II) observes in the plots of MR and BG, which exhibit positive Y intercepts. The IPD model of MR shows that the value of C increases with an increase in initial dye concentrations. It suggests that the boundary layer of adsorption gets thicker, and there is less potential for external mass transfer because of the higher concentration of dyes and more chance for internal mass transfer. The adsorption of BG dye exhibited similar observations. The IPD model provides valuable insights; it is often not the sole governing mechanism of adsorption kinetics. Other factors, such as external mass transfer, pore diffusion, and surface reactions, may also contribute to adsorption. It implies that the adsorption process is influenced by more than one stage, such as film diffusion and equilibrium adsorption. The diffusion coefficient is calculated from Eqn (12) to understand whether IPD is the rate-limiting step [41].

The diffusion coefficient value is 1.25 × 10-16 to 8.05 × 10-17 cm2/s, showing that IPD is not the rate-limiting step as it does not fall into the range of 10-11 to 10-13 cm2/s. Eqn (13) used to determine the film diffusion coefficient.

where r0 (cm) = radius of the adsorbent particle
Γ = film thickness (10−3 cm).
CS and CL = concentrations of dye in solid and liquid phases at a given time t.
t0.5 (min) = time required to complete the half of the adsorption

Film diffusion is the rate-controlling step if the value of DF is between 10-6 and 10-8 cm2/s. In this study, the values of DF for all concentrations of MR and BG dye are in the range of 1.07 × 10-6 to 8.62 × 10-7 and 1. 7 × 10-7 to 8.96 × 10-8, illustrating that film diffusion is the rate-controlling step.

Freundlich and Langmuir's model has been used to study the adsorption isotherm for MR (50–1000 mg/L) and BG (50–700 mg/L) with an adsorbent dosage of 0.02 g, pH 6 for MR and pH 3 for BG at constant temperature 30 °C. All the adsorption isotherm model parameters are present in Table S5 and Table S6. The Langmuir model is best fitting for the adsorption of MR as compared to Freundlich, having R2 value of 0.99 and χ2 value of 79.19. The R2 value of the Freundlich model is 0.838, and χ2 value is 4407.64 for the adsorption of MR. It suggests that monolayer adsorption of MR is taking place on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD with a maximum adsorption capacity of 1666.67 mg/g. However, as the temperature increases, the adsorption isotherm model inclined more toward the Freundlich model than the Langmuir model. The possible reason could be a decrease in adsorption capacity at higher temperatures because of the deterioration of active sites on the composite, which results in less bonding towards dye molecules and following multilayer adsorption. In contrast to MR adsorption, BG adsorption best fits with the Freundlich model with R2 value of 0.96–0.83 and χ2 value of 25.55–1846 at 303–333 K. This shows that multilayer adsorption is occurring in case of BG dye adsorption on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD at all temperature.

Traditional adsorption isotherms models like Langmuir and Freundlich can yield trustworthy findings for maximal adsorption capacity and affinity. However, these models do not offer crucial information for describing the adsorption mechanisms or pertinent insights into the adsorption dynamics. Thus, using statistical physical models to bridge this knowledge gap, dye adsorption has been investigated. To understand the adsorption of the dyes molecule on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD, non-linear curve fitting has been performed on experimental data at various temperatures. The monolayer adsorption and double-layer adsorption can be described by Eqn (14) and Eqn (15) (Li et al., 2019)

Where, C1/2 represents the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent, Ce is equlibirium concentration, C1 is the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent in first layer, C2 is the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent in second layer, nD is the number of dye molecules bonded to adsorption sites, and Dm is the density of functional groups. The non-linear curve fitting (statistical physical model) is shown in Fig. 12 at various temperature. According to the values of “nD” the literature has shown that there are three scenarios that might represent the adsorption orientation [42]:
i.
nD < 0 suggested a parallel adsorption direction means that the functional group of an adsorbent can accept a portion of the dye molecules (i.e., sharing occurs by two or more functional groups). As a result, the adsorption process involves multi interactions.
ii.
0.5 < nD < 1 means the dye molecules can be adsorbed in two distinct percentages simultaneously through both parallel and non-parallel directions (i.e., mixed orientations).
iii.
nD > 1 means adsorption of MV molecules occurring as non-parallel, and one adsorption site can take various dye molecules and form a multimolecular layer

In the case of MR adsorption, the value of nD at all temperatures is between 0.5–1, suggesting that mixed orientation, either parallel or non-parallel, has been occurring. This finding indicates that although anticipating a mixed orientation, the relevant adsorption active site captures some adsorbates. The ratio between parallel and non-parallel orientations can be calculated using the formula nD = P + (1 − P) 0.5, where P represents the percentage of non-parallel orientation, and 1-P represents the percentage of parallel orientation. Thus, 17 %, 35 %, 92 %, and 97 % of MR molecules are in a non-parallel orientation, while 82 %, 64.8 %, 7.6 %, and 2.6 %, respectively, are in a parallel orientation with an increase in temperature. The parallel orientation of MR molecules increases with an increase in temperature because, at higher temperatures, the composite's thermal stability reduces, reducing the active sites and thus reducing the dye adsorption. Also, at higher temperatures, the adsorption isotherm of MR dye inclined towards the Freundlich model, suggesting multilayer adsorption and, consequently, a fraction of parallel arrangement of MR dye molecules increases. The value of nD for BG dye adsorption was greater than 1, suggesting the adsorption of BG molecules occurring as non-parallel and one adsorption site can take various dye molecules and form a multimolecular layer.

The value of Qsat for MR and BG dye ranges from 2689-1648 mg/g and 2486–1670 mg/g with an increase in temperature from 30-45 °C and 30–60 °C. The findings in Table 3 demonstrate that MR has a substantially higher saturation adsorption capacity than BG. This result contributes to the potential interactions between the unique functional groups in the dyes and the adsorbents, enhancing adsorption efficiency. Additionally, the size of the chemical structure of MR dye plays a role in determining the mobility rate of the dye molecules, further impacting the adsorption process.

The impact of temperature provides insights into the spontaneity of the adsorption reaction and helps identify whether the adsorption mechanism is physical or chemical. Thermodynamic parameters such as Gibbs free energy (ΔG°), enthalpy (ΔH°), and entropy (ΔS°) are calculated to ascertain the spontaneity of the adsorption reaction, as outlined in Eqn (16) and (17)

According to the data in Table S7 and Table S8, the negative value of
Δ G
â ¸ °
is observed for all concentrations of MR and BG dyes. This suggests that the adsorption process occurs spontaneously and favourably at a specific temperature. Additionally, the negative values of
Δ H
â ¸ °
indicate an exothermic adsorption process, making it less favourable at higher temperatures. Furthermore, the negative values suggest a decrease in randomness of dye molecules on the EDA-CH-TiO2-CD surface. Adsorption generally leads to a decrease in the system's overall entropy (disorder). The dye molecules in the bulk solution exhibit a greater degree of freedom and randomness than when they are adsorbed on the surface of the adsorbent. Consequently, the decrease in entropy contributes to an exothermic process, as energy is released to reduce the system's disorder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption mechanism for adsorption of MR and BG dye on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The composite interaction with the dyes by adsorption greatly facilitates the inclusion of chitosan in the composite containing active sites. Surface adsorption, diffusion, chemisorption, and adsorption-complexation are the different mechanism methods that hold dye molecules to chitosan. According to the literature, adsorption-complexation is the most common idea for dye molecules adhering to chitosan [43]. Chemical bonding, aggregation processes, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic attractions, physical adsorption, ion exchange, van der Waals force, dye-dye interactions, and other interactions can occur simultaneously. The most common process is chemisorption (ion exchange, electrostatic attraction), with pH being the primary determinant of adsorption [43]. A powerful kind of adsorption called chemisorption involves the possible exchange of electrons but not of molecules.

As explained earlier, that pH = 6 is the most suitable and optimum value for the adsorption of MR dye, representing electrostatic attraction between CH-based composite and MR dye molecules. Moreover, the FTIR spectra of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD before and after adsorption of MR and BG dyes have been analysed to understand the complete adsorption mechanism as shown in Fig. 13 . FTIR spectra of MR dye consist of characteristic bands of –NH stretching groups, –CH vibration from CH3, C–C stretching vibration of benzene, N = N vibration at 3400, 2925, 1589, 1452 cm−1 wavenumber. Furthermore, multiple bands ranging from 1360-1050 cm−1 indicate the presence of C-N stretching groups. The increase in the characteristic band at 3400 cm−1 after dye adsorption represents more chelating binding sites, which the additional –OH groups created. The C = O-NHR groups of the crosslinked chitosan chains at 1646 cm−1 produce extra binding sites with the dye molecule and the sorption sites mentioned above, which are responsible for better adsorption behavior. This specific band in the chitosan composite corresponds to the vibration of C = O groups in the acetyl unit of C = O-NHR. This band's intensity increases following MR dye adsorption, suggesting the formation of a covalent bond between the dye and the adsorbent. Further, enhancement in the characteristic band of C-N stretching at 1382 cm−1 after adsorption of MR dye represents the hydrogen bonding between chitosan composite groups and nitrogen atoms of dye molecules. Thus, electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding can be a mechanism for adsorption of dye.

FTIR spectra of BG dye consist of characteristic bands of –NH and –CH aromatic elongation, S = O of sulfite group of dyed structure, –NH and –CH bending at 3500–3100 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, 1460–1337 cm−1. Moreover, characteristic bands from 1620-1590 cm−1 representing the C = C related to aromatic rings coupled with N = N azo group in dyed structure. After BG adsorption, the characteristic bands of 3400 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 increase due to hydrogen bonding between the nitrogen group of dye and the carboxyl group of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD. Further, a huge difference in the characteristic band of Ti-O-Ti at 695 cm−1 after BG adsorption is observed, representing electrostatic attraction between Ti-O and N+ of the dyed structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Column adsorption of dyes on EDA-CH-TiO2-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of an adsorbent in column adsorption research has been explored through the analysis of its breakthrough profile. Fig S17 (a) illustrates the MR dye and BG dye breakthrough profile of EDA-CH-TiO2-CD of column performed with 200 mg/L and 20 mg/L of initial dye concentration at pH 6 and pH 3 passed through a column at different flowrate like 0.2 mL/min, and 0.5 mL/min.

Initially, the feed dye solution of 200 mg/L and 20 mg/L passes through the inlet of the column; at this stage, the dye is adsorbed most swiftly and effectively by the upper few layers of the virgin composite. In Fig S17 (a), low levels of MR have been detected in the effluent up to 1000 min at an approximate flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. This result can be attributed to the greater number of sites on the composite. The MR dye concentration in effluents rises over time, following a smooth and steady climb in profile up to 4500 min for 0.2 mL/min flowrates and 3780 min for 0.5 mL/min flowrates. Since the composite is saturated due to the mass transfer zone (MTZ) descending to the column, the dye concentration in the effluent becomes equal to that in the influent. With the increase in mass transfer rate and quick MR dye elution, composite saturation happens faster at higher flow rates than at lower flow rates in the column study. However, in BG adsorption, the traces of dye in effluent are detected after 1720 min with the initial dye concentration of 20 mg/L and 0.2 mL/min flow rate. By 3180 min, the dye concentration at the inlet becomes equivalent to that at the outlet, indicating complete saturation of the adsorbent. The traces of dye effluents with 0.5 mL/min flowrate shows very early saturation of adsorbent which might be due to less residence time and slower diffusion of dye in the pores of adsorbent. The breakthrough profile was analyzed by various parameters as described by Eqn (19) to Eqn (22) [44,45].

Where L is the length of bed, ts and tb are saturation and breakthrough time, Cf is the concentration of dye in effluent, Co is the concentration of dye in inlet, and Q is the flowrate. Parameters obtained from breakthrough profile for both dyes are mentioned in Table 4 .

The composite is regenerated with 20 mL of 0.01 M HCl solution. The solvent passed through the column, and the effluent collected has been analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy. In the batch study, the composite is reusable for 8 adsorption–desorption cycles with a desorption efficiency of 99–91 % and an adsorption capacity of 550 mg/g – 450 mg/g. In the column adsorption study, the adsorbent is reusable 4–3 times for MR and BG adsorption, as depicted in Fig S17 (b). Early saturation of the composite as the regeneration cycle rises, possibly due to changes to the chemical and physical structure of the composite compared to the first cycle. Thus, the synthesized composite has the potential to be widely adopted in various textile industries for eliminating organic pollutants. Textile industry profit margins in developing countries like China typically range from 3–5 % annually and from 2–3 % in other developing countries such as India. The average EBITDA margin for top companies in India varies between 10 and 27 %. According to literature, due to this profitability the textile industries are only willing to invest in treatment processes if the cost is around $4-$6 per m3 for India and $0.4-$0.5 per ton for China [46]. By utilizing this composite material, textile industries can improve their wastewater treatment processes at an affordable cost, leading to enhanced environmental sustainability and compliance with regulations while reducing operational expenses.

The chitosan-based composite (EDA-CH-TiO2-CD) was developed for the removal of cationic and anionic dyes from synthetic wastewater. The incorporation of carbon dots onto TiO2 has been confirmed through FTIR, XPS, and UV–Vis/PL spectra analysis. Additionally, SEM/HRTEM analysis reveals the even dispersion of nanoparticles. Moreover, the modification of chitosan with EDA and ECH crosslinkers shows a higher degree of deacetylation and improves the adsorption performance of dyes as confirmed by FTIR analysis. The maximum adsorption capacity of composite for MR and BG dye was found to be 1666.67 mg/g and 1630 mg/g. Column adsorption studies revealed that the maximum bed adsorption capacity was 1284.35 mg/g for 200 mg/L of MR dye and 54.9 mg/g for BG dye at 0.2 mL /min. The adsorption validation results show multilayer adsorption and chemisorption processes. The findings of the statistical physical model analysis showed that the adsorption of dyes occurs by parallel and non-parallel orientation. FTIR results depict that electrostatic attraction, π-π interaction, and hydrogen bonding are the main mechanisms in the adsorption of dye onto composite. Machine learning results suggested that RF-based models demonstrated slightly superior performance compared to other models. It exhibited a greater ability to capture minor fluctuations in the variables under study. However, all algorithms delivered high-performing models suitable for modelling the process being studied except SVM. The predictive capability of machine learning algorithms relies on both the specific process being modelled and the available dataset. Also, the Pearson correlation was established to present the relationship between the output and input variables along with the support of the stochastic of the dataset, which needs the non-linear AI model to achieve the prediction with the highest accuracy. The study's Pearson correlation analysis reveals positive correlations between the composite's adsorption capacity and stirring speed, pH, and temperature for MR dye, while BG dye adsorption shows positive correlations with stirring speed, time, pH, and initial dye concentration.
